nativeRange.habitat: Calm to moderately-flowing headwaters and minor tributaries; substrates of sand, gravel, rocks, and boulders with leaf litter, submerged driftwood, and aquatic vegetation

diet.notes: Micropredator in the wild, feeding on small invertebrates and zooplankton. In captivity, generally accepts high-quality micro foods such as finely crushed flakes, micro pellets (under 0.5 mm), and powdered foods as a staple. Frozen or live daphnia, baby brine shrimp, cyclops, micro worms, and rotifers are well-regarded supplements. Food must be very small to fit their tiny mouths.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by BottomDog, 800x415. Tack-sharp side profile showing the vivid iridescent yellow-green lateral stripe against a dark planted background - textbook diagnostic view.

Photo by Julien Renoult, 2048x1536. Wild in-situ shot of a small group over a rocky stream bed - represents the schooling species in its native headwater habitat, though water is hazy and fish are distant.
Photo by Lerdsuwa, 800x500. Whole single fish in frame from a different angle; somewhat soft focus and duller coloration, kept only as a supplementary view.

summary: The Green Neon Rasbora (Microdevario kubotai) is a tiny schooling fish from Thailand and Myanmar, reaching just 1.5 to 2 cm as an adult. It displays a vivid iridescent yellow-green stripe along its flank, brightest against a dark substrate. Best kept in groups of eight or more in a planted, soft-water aquarium.
careNotes:
The Green Neon Rasbora is one of the smallest aquarium fish available in the hobby, reaching approximately 1.5–2 cm as an adult. It is native to well-oxygenated headwater streams in northwestern peninsular Thailand (Ranong and Phang Nga provinces, and Kanchanaburi and Tak provinces) and the Ataran River basin in southern Myanmar. Wild specimens are reported to occur in groups of 20–50 individuals, so a school of at least 8 is generally recommended to bring out confident, natural behavior in captivity.

The characteristic iridescent yellow-green lateral stripe shows most vividly against a dark substrate and background. This species is sometimes confused with Microrasbora erythromicron, the Emerald Dwarf Rasbora (now reclassified in the genus Danio), which has a different banding pattern and originates from Lake Inle in Myanmar. The Green Neon Rasbora belongs to the genus Microdevario rather than Boraras, though care requirements between these nanofish groups overlap considerably.

Taxonomy note: Modern taxonomy places this species in the family Danionidae (a group split off from the broader Cyprinidae), though aquarium hobby sources commonly list it under Cyprinidae.

Water quality: This species is generally regarded as intermediate in difficulty, owing to its sensitivity to elevated nitrates and sudden changes in water parameters. A biologically mature, well-established tank with stable water conditions is strongly advised. Weekly water changes of 30–50% are generally recommended to keep nitrates in check.

A 40 L tank with a 45 cm footprint can serve as a minimum for small schools in nano setups, but a larger aquarium is generally advised for groups of 10 or more.

The Green Neon Rasbora is widely considered safe to keep with adult-sized dwarf shrimp and is a commonly recommended shrimp-compatible tankmate. It may opportunistically consume shrimp fry or very small juvenile shrimp.
breedingNotes: Breeding the Green Neon Rasbora is generally considered moderately difficult. This species is an egg-scatterer with no parental care — adults tend to consume eggs if given the opportunity. Spawning generally occurs among fine-leaved aquatic plants or Java moss. Incubation has been reported to take approximately 72 hours, though exact timing varies with temperature. A dedicated breeding tank fitted with a fine mesh floor or a thick layer of moss is generally advised to protect eggs from being eaten. Fry are very small at hatching and require infusoria or similar microscopic foods initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms after a week or more. Breeding in captivity tends to occur fairly readily when conditioning fish are in good health and water quality remains stable.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary reference for taxonomy, native range, water parameters, tank requirements, and husbandry. Used as primary due to depth of scientific and care detail.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-check for care level, diet, group size (8–12), tank size (5-gallon minimum per article), temperament, and tankmate suggestions. Temperature listed as 72–80°F (22–27°C); pH 6–7 confirmed. No dedicated species page found; data drawn from Top 10 Rasboras article.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-check for water parameters (20–27°C, pH 6.0–7.0 confirmed), adult size (1.5–2 cm confirmed), lifespan (3–7 years), minimum tank (54 L / 15 gal), care level (intermediate to advanced noted), and breeding difficulty (moderate confirmed).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy cross-check: family listed as Danionidae (modern reclassification) vs Cyprinidae used by SeriouslyFish and aquarium hobby sources. Adult size confirmed at ~1.9 cm SL.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; no parental care; adults eat eggs. Breeding tank with fine mesh or Java moss recommended. Fry require microscopic foods initially.
